abstract: Capturing and recycling $CO_2$ into valuable chemicals like methanol is a promising strategy for mitigating greenhouse gas emissions. However, the thermodynamic stability of $CO_2$ makes its activation a significant kinetic bottleneck. While bulk gold is catalytically inert for hydrogenation, it can be transformed into a highly active catalyst when dispersed as nanoparticles on specific oxide supports. In this study, we combine experimental measurements and density functional theory (DFT) to demonstrate that gold nanoparticles anchored on a $CeO_x/TiO_2$ substrate exhibit exceptional activity for $CO_2$ conversion. We find that electronic polarization at the metal-oxide interface creates unique active centers that facilitate $CO_2$ adsorption and subsequent hydrogenation at unexpectedly low pressures. This synergistic effect not only boosts overall reaction rates but also significantly shifts selectivity toward methanol, even outperforming traditional copper-based catalysts. Our findings highlight how localized electronic properties at the gold-cerium interface can be engineered to drive efficient $CO_2$ utilization.
